chapters.title: Trump, Hannity, and the early COVID failures
chapters.text:
When COVID-19 struck the United States, the country needed a leader who would face the truth and listen to reason. President Donald Trump could not fill that role.

By March 26, 2020, over one thousand Americans had died from the virus, yet the President and Fox News were still in denial because they ignored the news. Dr. Colleen Smith recorded the inside of Elmhurst Hospital Center in Queens, New York, the President’s hometown, on her iPhone and shared the video with The New York Times. Sadly, the President never read the story because he had cut the White House subscriptions to the Times months earlier. Every attempt by Fox correspondents to report the truth ran into a wall.

Trump’s failure to grasp the urgency of the pandemic made things worse, and Fox News management backed his disbelief.

Rather than being a real friend to the President, Sean Hannity kept feeding him misinformation. On the night of March 26, 2020, Trump left the Chinese President on hold to call into Hannity’s show. The next day, Trump asked about the ratings. Hannity, as usual, fed him lies. The show started with 5.6 million viewers and ended with 5.4 million. People tuned in hoping the President would rise to the moment, and they were let down.

Instead of focusing on federal action and praising frontline health workers, Trump blamed Democrats for making the virus a political issue. He called the virus their newest hoax, comparing it to what he had labeled the impeachment hoax.

Among the reasons the US lagged in pandemic prep, the Trump-Fox feedback loop ranks high. Beyond Hannity, the Fox base kept backing Trump. Laura Ingraham called Democrats the panDEMic party, and on March 6, 2020, the network’s veteran medical commentator Dr. Marc Siegel said that in the worst case, the illness might resemble the flu.

Even though four out of five Fox viewers were over 55, and most were men, and COVID-19 hit men much harder, it is hard to say how many Fox loyalists died from the virus.

In the pages ahead, you will find a look at the rot at the core of our politics, the attack on a free and fair press, the gap between news and propaganda, and the gap between state media and the fourth estate.

chapters.title: How Fox News turned into a MAGA ad shop
chapters.text:
The frustration of current and former Fox staffers was so strong you could feel it. Stelter talked with over 140 staffers and 180 former staffers. Most agreed that Fox had become a danger to democracy.

Still, the network’s biggest fans compared it to a church, a senior center, and a city hall. A 2019 PRRI study pointed to a clear split between Fox News Republicans and other Republicans who did not lean mainly on Fox. Those who relied on Fox News were more tightly bound to Trump. A striking 55% said there was nothing Trump could do that would change their support. By contrast, just 29% of people who relied on other news sources felt that strongly.

Rupert and Lachlan Murdoch, father and son, take the blame for the loss of a free and fair press at Fox. They put profit ahead of real journalism. Sources told Stelter that the network was on track for two billion in profit. Things got so bad that James Murdoch, separated from Lachlan by only fifteen months, walked away from the family business out of disgust for what Fox News had become.

Top Fox leaders sometimes used money to silence dissent and buy people off, but money did not solve every problem.

Some staffers who valued press freedom over a paycheck left money on the table and chose their conscience. Megyn Kelly, for example, was offered over 25 million dollars a year for four years, yet she left for NBC on a three-year deal worth 23 million a year.

No TV network had ever shaped a president’s intelligence briefing or influenced federal hiring before. No president had ever championed a specific channel, taken private counsel from its hosts, or called for their firing when they disagreed. Fox News used to be conservative without being conspiratorial, and patriotic without being propagandist. But the Trump presidency’s bond with the network turned it into an ad shop for the MAGA agenda.

In one year, Matt Gertz of Media Matters noted Trump reacting at least 657 times to Fox News or Fox Business segments through tweets.

chapters.title: Trump’s media machine
chapters.text:
Rupert Murdoch brought Roger Ailes on board in 1996 to build a news channel. Ailes jumped at the chance for political and personal reasons. He wanted to stick it to his old bosses at NBC and to push Republican ideas on TV. At that point, Trump was busy buying buildings and running beauty pageants.

Ailes and Trump were close friends. They shared similar views. They came from the same generational references, with Ailes six years older. They held similar racist views on immigration and shared fears about crime.

For Trump, press and PR were the same thing. He took over New York media by serving as a steady source for gossip columnists. Using the fake name John Barron, he lied his way into the pages of respected magazines. That habit carried into his presidency. A member of Hannity’s inner circle said that whenever Hannity said sources close to the presidency, he meant the President.

Even as Trump warned Americans against anonymous sources, he was Hannity’s biggest anonymous source.

Trump was a serial liar, yet he had a crew of pro-Trump media operators ready to do his bidding.

Fox News started as a distinct voice, not as an extreme right outlet. After 9/11, it began beating CNN in the ratings. Over time, Fox News drifted further right and grew into its current shape. Through it all, Ailes pulled the strings, and Trump was bound to take advantage since Ailes owed him.

For Trump, the First Amendment was always about putting him first. If you ran pro-Trump news, you were good. If you crossed Trump, you were bad, then fake by 2016, then a hoax by 2018, as Brian Stelter once wrote.

TV ratings are measured by Nielsen. It monitors TVs in tens of thousands of homes and estimates total viewership from that sample.

chapters.title: TV ratings, scandals, and power plays
chapters.text:
Fox executives tolerated Trump for several reasons. One was that Rupert wanted access to the Oval Office, and backing Trump seemed like his best route. Ailes, who could have kept Trump in line, was already losing his political grip in his final years at Fox.

Whenever cable TV failed to produce content that helped him crush his rivals, Trump would rant about it. He accused Rupert of rigging a poll on February 17, 2016. He tried to cut Megyn Kelly from the Fox lineup to use her as a warning to anyone who would not back Trump fully.

Working in a toxic setting for money or status costs more than it gives back.

In the middle of all this, Ailes’s history of abuse finally caught up with him. Gretchen Carlson sued him on July 6, 2016. She exposed Ailes’s predatory behavior and kicked off a fight that fed the modern #MeToo movement.

The misconduct of Fox’s top brass began to surface. Carlson’s lawsuit cast light on Bill O’Reilly, Harvey Weinstein, and her co-host Steve Doocy. Reports of Doocy’s severe and widespread sexual harassment were never fully investigated, it seems.

Carlson’s tape recordings of her meetings with Ailes eventually brought the tyrant down.

Ailes did not go quietly. He got help. Trump and his old-school network of abusers stood by the accused. They used the same playbook that Ailes used to build Fox, that Hannity used to build his brand, and that Trump used to win the GOP primary, which was to attack.

Sadly for Ailes, James and Lachlan Murdoch, despite their different politics and views on how Fox News should run, agreed on one point. Ailes had become a cancer on the Fox body and had to be cut out. Both Murdoch sons had fought and lost to Ailes in earlier rounds.

Trump kept backing Ailes. He attacked Carlson’s character in public. He called her self-absorbed, flirtatious, and unpopular. Fox PR played its part too. It pushed out every clip of Carlson praising Ailes.

In some states, like New York, only one party needs to consent to a recording, which lets someone tape a conversation without telling the other person.

chapters.title: The cable TV power struggle
chapters.text:
Ailes refused to step down on his own. He kept acting like nothing was wrong. The Murdochs had to deactivate his badge. He eventually took 40 million dollars and a gag order in exchange for his immediate exit. The deal went public on July 21, 2016. The news almost stole the spotlight from Trump’s coronation in Cleveland.

With Ailes gone, the GOP became Trump’s party. At Fox, leaders again disagreed on the path forward. James Murdoch wanted to hire David Rhodes in the hope that Fox would move back toward the middle. That would mean more Shep Smith and less Sean Hannity. Rupert and Lachlan Murdoch liked Rhodes but feared changing direction. They saw it as business suicide.

Rupert took the wheel at Fox to keep the network stable until they could find a leader who would serve their interests. All kinds of misconduct from Ailes’s time came to light. Several lawsuits accusing Fox management of enabling Ailes kept the lawyers busy for over a year.

Hannity returned to a leading role now that Trump was President. Some believe Trump took his cues from what he heard on Hannity’s show. There were lines Ailes would not cross. Hannity blew past them by miles. Together with Trump, they sowed doubts about election security, spread lies about Hillary Clinton’s health, and did every kind of harm they could to their unsuspecting audience.

Inside Fox, Rupert, Shine, and Abernethy moved from Ailes’s top-down style to a more team-based model. Their aim was to keep things stable and profitable without disruption. The GOP’s TV arm was firmly under Trump’s grip, and it had strong support. Rudy Giuliani, Hannity, and Laura Ingraham served as his main lieutenants.

When a presidential candidate has a network at his beck and call, it can quietly tilt an election.

Rudy Giuliani, working with some Fox figures, reportedly pressured FBI director James Comey. He reopened an investigation into Hillary Clinton and made a public statement about it. It was an October surprise, with the election only weeks away. Given the sensitivity, he should have kept it quiet.

Even though the FBI cleared her, the Fox echo chamber convicted Clinton. They kept pairing the words Clinton and emails as the election drew near. Some political scientists say that move cost Clinton the election.

chapters.title: When polls mislead and channels pivot
chapters.text:
Shep Smith was the opposite of Sean Hannity. Smith wanted to tell it straight, while Hannity wanted to tell it his way. Smith stood for journalism while Hannity tore it down.

Fox, knowing Trump was behind in national polls, was already planning for a Clinton presidency by October 2016. The day of reckoning was close, and they wanted to brace for impact. Smith’s style of journalism was getting more space and attention. Rupert was starting to invest in real journalism and said he wanted Fox to be the best newsroom in America.

Like Trump and almost everyone else, Fox staffers went into Election Day expecting him to lose. Many anchors, line producers, and ad sales staff honestly wanted Clinton to win. Trump was, in a word, exhausting, as Brian Stelter once wrote.

People like Hannity did not find Trump exhausting. In fact, some think Hannity was Trump before Trump was Trump. The early exit polls looked grim for the Trump camp. Then the unexpected happened.

At 2:41 a.m., Fox News projected Trump as the President-elect. Hannity called it a modern-day political miracle. It was the night of November 8, 2016. Closet liberals at Fox cried while Trump backers partied until morning.

When Trump had to build his administration, he reached for Fox figures, since they were the people he knew best.

Journalistic integrity must come first for any news outlet with a conscience.

It was almost funny to see Fox management enforce a rule that no one could work for the network and for Trump at the same time. The cable channel had long shown, since Ailes’s time, that the two sides were tightly bound.

If there was any independent journalism left at Fox, it faded out after Trump’s win. Every journalist who chose integrity over a paycheck walked out the door.

chapters.title: How the media machine pulls the strings
chapters.text:
While some kept pushing Trump’s propaganda, Trump positioned himself as the media regulator. Only he could decide what counted as good media. Any news that backed his agenda was good media, and anyone who called out his lies was bad media. It was that direct.

Fox’s fear-driven appeals worked well on conservatives. In a Suffolk poll from October 2016, only 11% of Fox loyalists noticed the long-running economic recovery. A fear-based society was the perfect threshing floor for Trump.

Fake news was a term journalists and scholars first used to describe made-up stories on online platforms.

Hardly had Trump been elected President than he co-opted the term and started using it to attack any news that did not please him.

The Fox echo chamber pushed conservatives further to the right than liberals had moved to the left. Norm Ornstein and Thomas Mann, two DC think-tank scholars, called this asymmetric polarization.

The dangerous fallout from this kind of brainwashing turned deadly during the 2020 coronavirus outbreak. Former Reagan and Bush aide Bruce Bartlett said that Murdoch and the Fox brainwashing operation are risks to public health. Since Fox viewers had been told to trust only what came from Fox and had been trained to label other news sources as fake, it was hard for many to follow the rules to slow the virus.

The difference between the Ailes era and the post-Ailes era at Fox was that Ailes ran Fox in his time. After he left, there was a vacuum, and Trump filled it. On top of that, several Fox News personalities from the late 1990s and early 2000s won roles in Trump’s tight inner circle. Kellyanne Fitzpatrick, now known as Conway, became the President’s counselor, and Bill Shine became communications director. Monica Crowley was appointed assistant secretary at the treasury. Ann Coulter hammered into Trump’s ears the need to restrict immigration.

chapters.title: Final thoughts
chapters.text:
When Trump was elected President, he needed unbiased media more than ever to keep him from giving in to his worst instincts. Instead, he got fast-track access to the control room of one of America’s biggest cable networks, Fox.

The President was anything but presidential. He picked fights with reporters who challenged his version of the truth. He obsessed over TV ratings instead of producing results for the country. His mind wandered whenever the conversation was not about him. All of this built toward disaster, since his habits shaped America’s pandemic response. The President stayed in denial despite a flood of clear evidence.

Karma caught up with Trump’s enablers during the wave of protests after the murder of George Floyd. Fox’s programming became uneven. COVID-19 cases and deaths added to the pressure, and viral videos of police brutality sparked a nationwide protest movement unlike any before.

The real hoax in America has been the Trump era. The massive distortion of facts led by the Trump-Fox alliance wore many people out and turned them away from civic life. By rejecting balance and embracing conspiracy, Fox turned information into a weapon and a tool of manipulation. During the first half of 2020, Fox News used the word hoax over 900 times.

A few things you can try. Be deliberate about fact-checking the news and your own bias. One way is to balance what you watch. Never let any single channel fool you into treating it as your only source. It can radicalize you and make you think what it wants you to think.

Thanks for reading to the end.
